questions.question: Which Latin phrase expresses the maxim 'equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy'?

options.C: Ex turpi causa non oritur actio

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy' is expressed in the Latin phrase 'ubi jus ibi remedium', meaning 'where there is a right, there is a remedy'. This is the foundational maxim explaining why equity developed — to fill gaps left by the rigidity of the common law.

questions.question: The maxim 'equity follows the law' was established in which case?
questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity follows the law' derives from the Earl of Oxford's Case [1615]. It establishes that equity does not exist to overthrow or replace the common law but to supplement it where there is a gap or deficiency. Where the common law provides an adequate remedy, equity will not interfere.

questions.question: The doctrine of laches reflects which equitable maxim?

options.A: He who seeks equity must do equity

questions.explanation: The doctrine of laches reflects the maxim 'delay defeats equity'. It provides that if a party sits on their rights for too long, the court may refuse equitable relief. Laches is an equitable defence distinct from statutory limitation periods and can bar a claim even within the limitation period where delay has been unreasonable and prejudicial.

questions.question: The clean hands doctrine requires that the applicant's misconduct must be:

options.A: Of a criminal nature before the court will take notice
options.B: Connected to the specific transaction or relief sought
options.C: Disclosed to the respondent before proceedings are issued
options.D: Admitted in the pleadings to be raised as a defence

questions.explanation: Under the clean hands doctrine, the applicant's misconduct must be connected to the specific transaction or relief sought. General bad behaviour is not sufficient to deny equitable relief — the unclean hands must relate to the matter before the court. This was confirmed in Moody v Cox and applied in the Irish case of Murphy Buckley and Keogh v Pye Ireland.

questions.question: The rule in Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] illustrates which equitable maxim?

options.C: Equity regards as done that which ought to be done
options.D: He who comes to equity must come with clean hands

questions.explanation: The rule in Walsh v Lonsdale [1882] illustrates the maxim 'equity regards as done that which ought to be done'. In that case, an agreement for a lease was treated as equivalent to an actual lease in equity. The maxim allows equity to look at substance over form — where a party has an obligation to do something, equity will treat it as already done.

questions.question: In Feeney v MacManus [1937], which equitable maxim was applied to divide surplus funds equally among members?

options.A: Equity will not perfect an imperfect gift
options.D: He who seeks equity must do equity

questions.explanation: Feeney v MacManus [1937] illustrates the maxim 'equality is equity'. Where there is no basis for distinguishing between competing claims, equity will divide equally. In this case, surplus funds of a dissolved unincorporated association were divided equally among existing members.

questions.question: The maxim 'equity acts in personam' means that the Court of Chancery historically enforced its orders against:

options.A: The property of the defendant directly through sequestration
options.B: The defendant personally, through their conscience
options.C: Third parties who held assets on behalf of the defendant
options.D: The Crown as ultimate enforcer of equitable obligations

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity acts in personam' means that the Court of Chancery enforced its orders not against property directly but against the person — the defendant's conscience. Disobeying an order of the Chancellor resulted in personal penalties, including imprisonment for contempt. This personal jurisdiction gave equity its unique character.

questions.question: Which maxim underpins the doctrines of satisfaction and ademption?

options.A: Equity will not suffer a wrong to be without a remedy
options.B: Where the equities are equal, the first in time prevails
options.C: Equity imputes an intention to fulfil an obligation
options.D: Equity looks to the intent rather than the form

questions.explanation: The maxim 'equity imputes an intention to fulfil an obligation' underpins the doctrines of satisfaction and ademption. It means that where a person is under an obligation and subsequently does an act referable to that obligation, equity will presume they intended to fulfil it.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes the relationship between equity and the common law?

options.A: Equity displaces the common law wherever there is a conflict
options.B: Equity supplements the common law by filling gaps and remedying deficiencies
options.C: Equity and common law operate as entirely separate systems with no interaction
options.D: Equity only applies in criminal proceedings where statutory penalties are inadequate

questions.explanation: Equity supplements the common law by filling gaps and remedying deficiencies — it does not overthrow or replace it. The maxim 'equity follows the law' establishes this relationship. Equity only acts where the common law provides an inadequate remedy.

questions.question: The maxim 'he who seeks equity must do equity' requires the applicant to:

options.A: Have obtained independent legal advice before applying to the court
options.B: Be prepared to act fairly and deal justly with the opposing party
options.C: Pay costs in advance as a condition of receiving equitable relief
options.D: Waive any right to damages at common law before seeking an injunction

questions.explanation: 'He who seeks equity must do equity' requires that if a party is asking the court for an equitable remedy, they must be prepared to act fairly and deal justly with the other party. It is a condition of equitable relief — the applicant cannot seek the court's assistance while behaving unconscionably towards the respondent.
